title: A Short History of Colombia
subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 13 eras that shaped it.

items.label: First Peoples
items.summary: Hunter-gatherers and early agriculturalists populate the Colombian landscape.
items.text: The first humans arrived in present-day Colombia during the final ice age, following game and establishing settlement patterns across diverse ecosystems from the Caribbean coast to the Andean highlands. Archaeological evidence from sites like El Abra and Pubenza shows these early inhabitants gradually transitioned from nomadic hunting to semi-permanent settlements and the cultivation of maize, cassava, and potatoes. Pottery production emerged around 3,000 BC, marking a shift toward more complex societies. By 1,500 BC, distinct regional cultures began developing in response to their environments.

items.label: Regional Chiefdoms
items.summary: Sophisticated regional societies flourish with agriculture, hierarchy, and distinctive crafts.
items.text: Across Colombia's varied geography, organized chiefdoms emerged with recognized leaders, stratified societies, and shared cultural practices. In the highlands, the Herrera Period (1,270 BC–800 BC) saw the development of agricultural villages with increasing social complexity. The Muisca and Chibcha peoples in the highlands developed terrace farming and settled confederation systems. Coastal groups like the Tayrona created elaborate settlements and trade networks. These societies developed distinctive pottery styles, astronomical knowledge, and religious practices centered on nature worship and ancestor veneration.

items.label: Pre-Columbian Confederations
items.summary: Powerful indigenous confederations reach their peak with complex governance and impressive infrastructure.
items.text: By 1,200 AD, the Muisca Confederation in the highlands had become a sophisticated state with two competing kingdoms: Bacatá (Bogotá) and Hunza (Tunja). The Tayrona civilization on the Caribbean coast built the spectacular stone city of Ciudad Perdida, demonstrating advanced architectural and agricultural knowledge. These societies organized labor for massive public works, maintained trade networks across vast distances, and developed calendrical systems for religious ceremonies. The Quimbaya civilization in the western valleys was renowned for its extraordinary gold work, which would later captivate Spanish conquistadors.

items.label: Spanish Conquest
items.summary: Spanish conquistadors overwhelm indigenous confederations and claim territory for the Crown.
items.text: Gonzalo Jiménez de Quesada led an expedition up the Magdalena River in 1537, reaching the Muisca highlands and conquering the confederation through a combination of military force, disease, and indigenous alliances. The conquest was brutally efficient despite some organized resistance from indigenous leaders like Tisquesusa. Quesada founded Bogotá in 1538 on the site of Bacatá, establishing Spanish administrative control over the highlands. Within a generation, the demographic catastrophe of European diseases had decimated the indigenous population, who lacked immunity to smallpox and other pathogens.

items.label: Colonial Foundation
items.summary: Spanish establish the Viceroyalty of New Granada with encomienda labor and Catholic conversion.
items.text: Spain organized its Colombian territories as part of the Viceroyalty of Peru, later creating the separate Viceroyalty of New Granada in 1717. The colonial economy was built on the encomienda system, which granted Spanish settlers control over indigenous labor in exchange for Christian instruction. Gold mining became the primary economic driver, with enslaved Africans increasingly imported to replace the decimated indigenous workforce. Cartagena developed into a major Caribbean port for slave trading and colonial commerce, while Bogotá became the administrative center of the highlands.

items.label: Mature Colony
items.summary: A stratified, plantation-based colonial society develops with African slavery and Spanish creole dominance.
items.text: Over the 18th century, Colombia's colonial economy matured around sugar, tobacco, and gold exports, increasingly dependent on enslaved African labor in lowland regions while highland areas used indigenous tribute labor. A rigid caste system emerged with Spanish-born peninsulares at the top, followed by criollos (Spanish-descended colonists born in America), then free people of color, indigenous peoples, and enslaved Africans at the bottom. The Viceroyalty's borders expanded to include Venezuela and Ecuador, creating a vast administrative region. Intellectual currents from the Enlightenment began circulating among educated creoles, fostering resentment of Spanish monopolies and political exclusion.

items.label: Independence Wars
items.summary: Colombia joins Spanish American independence movements, achieving freedom through brutal warfare.
items.text: The independence struggle began in 1810 when Bogotá's creole elite declared autonomy, though Spanish forces retained control of major regions. Simón Bolívar emerged as the decisive military leader, liberating Venezuela and then invading New Granada from the east, defeating Spanish forces at the Battle of Boyacá in 1819. Bolívar united the liberated territories into Gran Colombia, a massive confederation including present-day Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, and Panama. Complete Spanish capitulation came in 1825, but the new nation immediately faced challenges from regional rivalries, economic collapse, and the integration of populations with vastly different interests.

items.label: National Formation
items.summary: Colombia struggles through dozens of civil wars as liberals and conservatives battle for national direction.
items.text: Gran Colombia collapsed in 1830, and the Republic of Colombia faced seven major civil wars between competing ideological factions and regional powers. Conservatives sought a centralized state with Church authority, while Liberals demanded federalism and secular governance. The Constitution of 1863 created the United States of Colombia as a radical federal system that proved ungovernable, with nearly autonomous states. Bloody conflicts erupted repeatedly over tariffs, religious power, and regional autonomy. Economic stagnation, failed attempts at modernization, and the persistence of slavery (finally abolished in 1851) deepened social divisions.

items.label: The Thousand Days War
items.summary: A devastating three-year civil war kills hundreds of thousands and destroys Colombia's economy.
items.text: The War of the Thousand Days erupted when Conservatives reimposed centralized government, prompting Liberal insurrection across the nation. The conflict killed an estimated 100,000 people in a nation of just four million, with entire regions depopulated by violence and disease. The war devastated Colombia's already fragile economy, destroyed infrastructure, and left the country bankrupt and exhausted. Conservative victory produced the 1903 Constitution, which reduced federalism but failed to address underlying grievances. The war's conclusion left Colombia impoverished, divided, and vulnerable to foreign intervention.

items.label: Twentieth-Century Transformation
items.summary: Colombia modernizes under Conservative stability, but loses Panama and faces renewed social tensions.
items.text: The Conservative hegemony that began in 1903 provided relative peace and order, enabling infrastructure development and modest economic growth centered on coffee exports. However, the nation lost Panama in 1903 when the United States backed Panamanian independence to build the Canal, a trauma that defined Colombian foreign policy for decades. Coffee became the nation's economic engine, creating new wealth but also a vulnerable dependency on export markets. The oligarchic political system excluded workers and peasants, leading to strikes, agrarian unrest, and the rise of socialist and communist movements that alarmed the elite.

items.label: La Violencia
items.summary: The assassination of a popular Liberal leader sparks a decade of sectarian terror killing hundreds of thousands.
items.text: The 1948 murder of Liberal presidential candidate Jorge Eliécer Gaitán unleashed La Violencia, a period of savage partisan warfare that claimed between 200,000 and 300,000 lives over ten years. Conservatives and Liberals formed armed bands that committed mass atrocities against civilians, displacing rural populations and destroying entire communities. The violence was not merely political but deeply rooted in land disputes, economic inequality, and the state's inability to maintain order. Military governments attempted to restore peace through force, but the underlying rural and social conflicts festered. By 1958, Conservatives and Liberals agreed to alternate the presidency under the National Front, a power-sharing agreement that restored stability but excluded other political forces.

items.label: Cold War Instability
items.summary: Political reforms bring democratic form but persistent violence, drug trafficking, and guerrilla insurgency destabilize the nation.
items.text: The National Front restored democratic forms, but excluded Colombians from parties outside the Conservative-Liberal duopoly, driving frustrated groups toward armed rebellion. Marxist guerrillas, especially the FARC (founded 1964) and ELN, gained strength in rural areas by claiming to represent peasants and the poor. By the 1970s, the drug trade—particularly cocaine production—emerged as a parallel economy that financed both guerrillas and right-wing paramilitaries, creating a complex web of violence. The Medellín Cartel under Pablo Escobar exported cocaine globally while terrorizing Colombia with bombings and assassinations. Political assassinations, disappearances, and massacres became routine as the state struggled to maintain legitimacy against multiple armed threats.

items.label: Democratic Transition
items.summary: Colombia faces down drug trafficking and internal conflict, gradually strengthening democracy despite ongoing violence.
items.text: The government's defeat of the Medellín Cartel and capture of Pablo Escobar in 1993 marked a turning point, removing the most destabilizing force from Colombian society. The 1991 Constitution established a modern legal framework with greater checks and balances, and successive governments pursued negotiations with guerrillas alongside military campaigns. President Álvaro Uribe's hardline approach from 2002 to 2010 significantly degraded guerrilla capacity through military operations, though at great cost to civilians. His successor Juan Manuel Santos pursued the opposite strategy, negotiating peace with the FARC and signing a historic agreement in 2016 that ended the 52-year conflict, though implementation remains contested. Today Colombia has achieved relative peace and democratic stability, though inequality, drug trafficking, and the integration of former combatants remain significant challenges.
